meta.title: Words Unlocked 2026
meta.subtitle: A national poetry program and contest for incarcerated youth

theme.name: To Let Go
theme.definition: Sometimes holding on feels safer. But real strength can live in release. To let go means to make peace with what we can't change, to forgive what hurt us, and to open space for something new to grow. It's not giving up, it's growing up. It's learning that freedom often starts with an open hand.

sometimes-tank-ball.title: Sometimes
sometimes-tank-ball.author: Tarriana "Tank" Ball
lines: Sometimes
I'm afraid to hold on
Because I'm afraid to let go

harlem-hughes.title: Harlem
harlem-hughes.author: Langston Hughes
lines: What happens to a dream deferred?
Does it dry up like a raisin in the sun?
Or fester like a sore— and then run?
Does it stink like rotten meat?
Or crust and sugar over— like a syrupy sweet?
Maybe it just sags like a heavy load.
Or does it explode?

metaphor-original-1.title: My Phone
metaphor-original-1.author: BreakFree (sample)
lines: My phone is a window I keep cleaning.
My phone is a mirror I keep angry.
My phone is a door that opens only outward.
My phone is a friend with a quiet name.

personification-original-1.title: The Streetlight
personification-original-1.author: BreakFree (sample)
lines: The streetlight clears its throat at dusk
and tells its same old joke to the moths.
The moths laugh in tiny circles.
Nobody but me is listening.

alliteration-original-1.title: Bricks
alliteration-original-1.author: BreakFree (sample)
lines: Brother breaks the brick, builds the bridge,
borrows back the boy he used to be.
Brave is not a building. Brave is the brick.
Brave is the boy, building.

onomatopoeia-original-1.title: Block Party
onomatopoeia-original-1.author: BreakFree (sample)
lines: Boom — the speakers learn a new word.
Crackle — the foil unwraps the chicken.
Pop — the can confesses.
Shh — the baby agrees with the music.

spoken-word-original-1.title: Mic Check
spoken-word-original-1.author: BreakFree (sample)
lines: One. Two. One. Two.
If you can hear me — I have been quiet for a long time.
If you can hear me — I am still here.
If you can hear me — I am not done.
Check. Check. Check.

lessons.title: Unit Launch & Poetry Introduction
objectives: Analyze poems in literature circles to answer the question "What is poetry?"
Synthesize information from readings in literature circles and present findings
keyPoints: Today is the first day of the poetry unit.
Students will work in small groups (literature circles) with assigned roles.
Groups analyze why poems are written and what techniques they use.

activities.title: Define Poetry
activities.prompt: Write your own definition of poetry. What makes a piece of writing a poem?

activities.title: Unit Launch
activities.content: Introduce the Words Unlocked unit: focus poets, contest theme "To Let Go," the 7-lesson arc, and the culminating Poetry Slam. Read one poem from a focus poet aloud and model annotation.

activities.title: Literature Circles Setup
activities.content: Explain literature circle roles: Reader, Discussion Leader, Summarizer, Presenter. Each group gets two poems pre-selected by the teacher and a guided analysis sheet.

activities.title: Literature Circle Work
activities.content: Groups read assigned poems, complete the analysis handout, and build a poster to present back to the class.

activities.title: Group Sheet Turn-in
activities.prompt: Submit the group literature circle analysis sheet. Each student rates the group's collaboration on the reflection sheet.

standards.description: Cite textual evidence to support analysis

differentiation: Support during literature circle for struggling readers

lessons.title: Metaphor & Simile
objectives: Identify similes and metaphors within a poem and analyze the significance of their use
Differentiate between similes and metaphors
Create original similes and metaphors
keyPoints: A simile is a comparison using 'like' or 'as'.
A metaphor is a comparison that calls one thing by the name of another.
Good readers don't just identify these — they ask "why is this comparison significant?"

activities.title: Find the Comparison
activities.prompt: Read the following sentences. Identify what two things are being compared and explain what the author means by the comparison.

activities.title: Metaphor & Simile
activities.content: Define metaphor and simile. Students record definitions and examples in their figurative language dictionary.

activities.title: Analyze "Happiness"
activities.content: Read the first stanza of "Happiness" together. Model: identify the figurative language, name the two things compared, explain significance.

activities.title: Analyze + Compose
activities.content: Students analyze "Harlem" using the graphic organizer, then write an original 8-line poem comparing two things. Sentence starters available for support.

activities.title: Define Both
activities.prompt: 1. What is a metaphor? 2. What is a simile?

differentiation: Sentence starters for the independent composition

lessons.title: Personification
objectives: Identify personification in poems
Analyze how personification creates meaning
Write original personification
keyPoints: Personification gives human qualities to non-human things.
It creates vivid imagery and emotional resonance.

activities.title: Flocabulary Video
activities.prompt: Watch the Flocabulary video on personification. Write down two examples you notice.

activities.title: Personification
activities.content: Define personification with examples. Add to the figurative language dictionary.

activities.title: Analyze a Poem
activities.content: Read a poem rich in personification together. Identify each instance and discuss what it makes the reader feel.

activities.title: Write Your Own
activities.content: Students write an 8-line poem using personification to bring an object or place to life.

activities.title: Best Line
activities.prompt: Share the best line of personification from your poem. Why is it your favorite?

lessons.title: Alliteration & Symbolism
objectives: Identify alliteration and symbolism in poems
Analyze how each device contributes to meaning
Write original lines using both devices
keyPoints: Alliteration is the repetition of initial consonant sounds.
Symbolism uses concrete objects to stand for abstract ideas.

activities.title: What do the images mean?
activities.prompt: Look at the three images on the slide. What ideas or feelings might each one symbolize?

activities.title: Alliteration & Symbolism
activities.content: Define alliteration and symbolism with examples. Add to the dictionary.

activities.title: Analyze Poems
activities.content: Read two short poems — one heavy in alliteration, one heavy in symbolism. Annotate together.

activities.title: Write Your Own
activities.content: Students write an 8-line poem using at least one instance of alliteration and one symbol.

activities.title: Name the Symbol
activities.prompt: Identify the symbol in your poem and explain what it represents.

standards.description: Analyze the impact of rhymes and other repetitions of sounds

differentiation: Image-based entry point for visual learners

lessons.title: Onomatopoeia & Imagery
objectives: Identify onomatopoeia and imagery in poems
Analyze how sensory detail builds tone and meaning
Compose lines rich in sound and sensory imagery
keyPoints: Imagery: language that appeals to the senses.

activities.title: Describe a Scene
activities.prompt: Describe a place you know well using all five senses. No emotions allowed — only what you see, hear, smell, taste, touch.

activities.title: Onomatopoeia & Imagery
activities.content: Define onomatopoeia and imagery. Listen to a recording of a poem rich in both.

activities.title: Analyze Poems
activities.content: Read two poems together. Highlight every sense the poet activates.

activities.title: Write Your Own
activities.content: Students write a poem that uses at least one onomatopoeia and engages at least three of the five senses.

activities.title: Read Aloud
activities.prompt: Read your favorite line out loud to a partner. Which sense did you activate?

standards.description: Determine the meaning of words and phrases including figurative meanings

lessons.title: Spoken Word
objectives: Identify what makes spoken-word poetry distinct from page poetry
Practice performance techniques (pace, volume, breath, gesture)
Begin drafting a spoken-word piece on the theme
keyPoints: Spoken word is poetry made for performance.
It centers voice, rhythm, and presence.
It often takes on social, personal, and political themes.

activities.title: Name That Device
activities.prompt: On the slide are five short lines of poetry. Name the device in each (metaphor, simile, personification, alliteration, imagery).

activities.title: Spoken Word & Slam
activities.content: Show two short spoken-word video clips. Discuss what makes them work: pacing, repetition, breath, the body. Introduce performance techniques.

activities.title: Draft Your Piece
activities.content: Students draft a spoken-word piece engaging the year's theme of "To Let Go." Encourage repetition, direct address, and rhythm.

activities.title: One Line, Out Loud
activities.prompt: Stand and read one line of your draft aloud. Pay attention to your breath.

standards.description: Adapt speech to a variety of contexts and tasks

differentiation: Allow students to perform to a partner first

lessons.title: Poetry Recital / Poetry Slam
objectives: Perform an original poem with intentional voice and presence
Listen to peers with attention and respect
Provide constructive, specific feedback
keyPoints: Audience signals (snaps, applause) are part of the form.

activities.title: Relaxation
activities.prompt: Three deep breaths. Read your poem silently to yourself once.

activities.title: Recital Expectations
activities.content: Set norms for the recital: how performers are introduced, how the audience responds, how feedback works. Show an example from a focus poet.

activities.title: Poetry Slam / Recital
activities.content: Each poet is introduced by name and takes the stage to perform. After the round, peers offer one piece of specific, kind feedback per performer.

activities.title: One Thing I'll Keep
activities.prompt: Name one thing you'll carry forward from this unit — a line, a technique, a feeling, anything.

standards.description: Adapt speech to a variety of contexts and tasks

differentiation: Option to perform seated or read off the page

handouts.title: Lesson 1 — Do Now
handouts.body: Write your own definition of poetry. What makes a piece of writing a poem? Be specific and use at least two complete sentences.

handouts.title: Lesson 2 — Do Now
handouts.body: Read the following sentences. First identify what two things are being compared and then explain what the author means by the comparison.

handouts.title: Lesson 3 — Do Now
handouts.body: Watch the Flocabulary video on personification. Write down two examples of personification you notice from the video.

handouts.title: Lesson 4 — Do Now
handouts.body: Look at the three images on the board. For each image, write down what idea, feeling, or concept it might symbolize.

handouts.title: Lesson 5 — Do Now
handouts.body: Describe a place you know well using all five senses (sight, sound, smell, taste, touch). No emotions allowed — only what you sense directly.

handouts.title: Lesson 6 — Do Now
handouts.body: On the board are five short lines of poetry. Name the poetic device used in each: metaphor, simile, personification, alliteration, or imagery.

handouts.title: Lesson 7 — Do Now
handouts.body: Three deep breaths. Read your poem silently to yourself once. Underline the line you are most proud of.
